lines.text: Welcome back to PRGE. Caroline Chen with weather. It's {{CLOCK}} Madison time, {{ELAPSED}} since the Purge commenced.
Holden Peplinski here with analytics. We're tracking live. Current neighborhood distribution shows East Side carrying 34 percent of activity.
That's already elevated. Last year at this hour it was running 28.

Correct. If you visualize the board — and I mean this literally, like a chessboard — the East Side is the center rank right now. Capitol Square is attempting to castle kingside, but the perimeter's holding.
Holden.
Right. Data. Current confirmed count is 38. We're at 38 for the full metro. That's 19 above the same-hour 2168 benchmark.
Nineteen above. Say that again for the listeners.
Last year at {{CLOCK}}, we had 19 confirmed kills. Tonight: 38. We've doubled the pace.
People are listening to that number. Understand what that means.
The methodology matters. We're pulling from police scanners, hospital admissions, direct field reports. The data is reliable. The pace is not a statistical artifact.
Weather is cooperating with that pace. Clear skies, SW winds at 8, visibility unlimited. No weather suppression tonight.

Which brings me to the wind dynamics. Southwest at 8 mph is actually suppressing some activities — it's pushing environmental toxins east, which inhibits visibility-dependent targeting in the Beltline corridor.
You're saying the wind is saving lives.
I'm saying that if the wind were north or west at the same speed, we'd be looking at a 12-to-14 percent increase over the current already-elevated baseline.
So we're lucky. Tonight.

We're statistically fortunate in one vector. In others, we're not. Williamson Street barricade is holding at the Yahara bridge, but the coordination of the activity on East Side suggests something organized. You don't see 34 percent of total activity concentrated without coordination.
Coordinated predation.
That's the inference, yes. Which means the pieces are moving together. Like a passed pawn structure in the middle game — they're not random. They're advancing.
Stop with the chess. The listeners don't need metaphors right now.

Fair. Direct statement: we're tracking organized group activity. At least two confirmed group movements on East Side. One is a mobile unit — they're moving through Schenk's Corners with apparent tactical coordination. The other is a static perimeter.
Static where.
Atwood Avenue corridor. They've established what looks like a checkpoint between Wingra and Bluff Street. Field reports suggest they're screening foot traffic.
Screening for what.
Unknown. The field reports are fragmentary. But the pattern suggests resource control — they're not killing indiscriminately. They're filtering.
That's worse.
In terms of predictability and danger escalation, yes. Random violence has a ceiling. Organized violence has momentum.
How confident are you in the coordination assessment.
Seventy-eight percent. I'm excluding anomalies. But the spatial clustering doesn't happen by accident at this density and timeline.
Okay. Listeners in the East Side — if you're hearing this, you already know. Lock your doors. Reinforce your barricades. Don't open them for anyone.
The Williamson Street barricade is proof of concept. That perimeter is holding because it's organized. It's armed. It's disciplined.
And you're suggesting.
That if you have a neighborhood watch — if you have the capacity to organize — tonight is the night. This is not random drift. This is coordinated activity. Defense works.
We're {{ELAPSED}} into the Purge. Weather holds clear. Temperature 41 degrees and dropping. Wind southwest at 8. No precipitation forecast until hour 4, and that's just drizzle.
The drizzle doesn't change the dynamic. If anything, it'll reduce visibility further, which suppresses the coordinated movement.
So we get four hours of escalation, then the weather gives us a break.
If the pattern holds. But the data is suggesting the pattern won't hold. The coordinated activity is aggressive. I'd expect them to accelerate into the window, not wait for weather suppression.
You're saying they'll move harder in the next two hours.
I'm saying the logic of coordinated predation suggests front-loading the violence. Get the kill count up in the clear window, establish territorial control, then hold it.
And we're at 38 already. Double the pace.
Which means we could be looking at 60-to-70 by {{CLOCK}} next hour.
That would be the worst two-hour window we've seen in five years.
It would. But it's not unprecedented. The 2163 Purge saw 67 kills in hours 2-to-3. That was the year of the Northside Coalition.
Which disbanded.
After Purge night. They lost organizational coherence. Attrition. Fragmentation. But during the night, they ran a unified operation.
And we're seeing that again.
The spatial and temporal data suggests it. I'm at 78 percent confidence, which means there's still a 22 percent chance I'm pattern-matching noise.
But you're not usually wrong about pattern.
I'm not usually wrong about pattern. No.
Listeners, you heard that. Plan accordingly. We'll have updates at the top of the next hour.
I want to note something else. The kill rate is elevated, but the method distribution is showing a shift. Last year, 44 percent of kills were firearm-based. Currently, we're tracking 52 percent.
That's ammunition. They have more ammunition.
Or they're better supplied in this particular cell. Which circles back to organization. You don't see coordinated ammunition distribution without infrastructure.
Infrastructure implies planning. Weeks of planning.
Months, possibly. This didn't assemble in the last few days.
So someone knew this was coming. The government knew?
I can't speak to government knowledge. I can speak to data. The data says coordinated, equipped, aggressive cell activity. The government's oversight responsibility is their problem.
It's our problem. It's everyone's problem. 38 people are already dead.
Yes.
And there are ten more hours.
There are ten more hours. And if the pattern holds, the next two of those hours will be the worst.
Can you model for us what worst looks like.

Worst looks like 80-to-90 kills by hour 4. It looks like territorial consolidation across East Side and Capitol Square. It looks like the coordinated cell moving into South Side or Willy Street if the perimeter there breaks.
And best case.
Best case is the neighborhood watch holds. The cold front arrives early. Visibility drops. The coordinated cell loses effectiveness. We flatten the curve and end the night at 150 to 170 total.
150 to 170. That's still 60 to 80 higher than last year.
Yes. Even best case, this is a worse night than 2168.
People need to understand that. This is not a normal year.
No. This is not a normal year. The Purge has a trajectory. Some years flatten early. Some years escalate. This year is escalating.
Stay home. Stay locked. Don't open your doors. We're monitoring. We're here all night.
We are. And we'll update you as the data shifts.
This is Caroline Chen. Weather update coming in sixty seconds. Holden's staying with us for the next hour.
I'll be here. And I'll be honest with you: I'm concerned. The data suggests we should all be concerned.
Temperature 41, SW wind 8, visibility unlimited. Clear skies until hour 4. After that, drizzle. Wind picks up to 12 by midnight.
Which will suppress the mobile unit's effectiveness. If they're moving fast, they'll want to finish their operation before the visibility drops.
So two hours of hard push, then weather relief.
That's the pattern I'm tracking. Two hours of coordinated maximum effort. Then fatigue and weather suppression set in.
And after that.
After that, it depends on whether they've established territorial control. If they have, they'll defend it. If they don't, they'll fragment and the night becomes more random.
Random is better.
Random is better. Organized violence has momentum. Random violence has a ceiling.
We hope for the ceiling.
We hope for the ceiling.
Listeners, if you're just tuning in, welcome to Purge Radio. Quinn will be back in the next hour with longer-form analysis. Right now it's weather and data. Stay with us.
And if you're in the East Side, remember: the Willy Street barricade is holding. Organization works. Community works. If you can reach a barricade, reach it.
But don't expose yourself. If your neighborhood isn't organized yet, stay locked inside. Don't try to move through active zones.
The data on attempted evacuation is grim. 73 percent of people who try to move through active zones don't make it to their destination.
Stay home. That's the message.
Stay home. Stay locked. Stay quiet.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide confirmed kills: 38. Hour 2 total: 19 (double pace vs. 2168). East Side carrying 34% of activity.

ALERT: East Side (Schenk's Corners / Atwood Ave) showing coordinated group activity. Mobile unit + checkpoint established Atwood/Wingra-Bluff corridor. Williamson Street barricade holding at Yahara River bridge.

Coordinated cell assessment: 78% confidence. Multiple equipped tactical units. Ammunition distribution elevated (52% of kills firearm-based vs. 44% 2168 avg). Projected worst-case: 80-90 kills by hour 4.

WEATHER WINDOW CLOSING: Clear skies until {{CLOCK}}+2 hours. Then drizzle, wind increase to 12 mph. Coordinated cell likely to accelerate operations in clear window. Two hours of maximum activity forecast.
